petitioner_background: The petitioner is a performer (references to 'performances' in the record) from Nepal who sought classification as an alien of extraordinary ability under Section 203(b)(1)(A) of the INA. The specific field is not clearly identified in the decision text, though the petitioner references certificates of accomplishment and a 1990 Junior Diploma documenting passage of an examination. At some point in the proceedings the petitioner claimed to meet nine of the ten regulatory categories of evidence.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted evidence of a (redacted) award referenced in a letter from the Embassy of Nepal. On motion, the petitioner submitted a copy of the Embassy of Nepal letter (previously quoted on appeal) and a March 27, 2013 letter referencing 'numerous certificates for [the petitioner's] accomplishments.' The petitioner had abandoned all other honors on appeal except the one (redacted) award.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found that even if the 2010 award were qualifying (which it was not), the plain language of the criterion requires more than one award or prize. The copy of the previously quoted Embassy of Nepal letter did not satisfy 8 C.F.R. § 103.5(a)(2) because it only addresses one award. The March 27, 2013 letter's vague reference to 'numerous certificates' did not help because the petitioner had abandoned claims to any nationally or internationally recognized awards other than the one (redacted) award. Evidence offered for the first time before the AAO after being put on notice of deficiencies during the RFE stage will not be accepted per Matter of Soriano and Matter of Obaigbena.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: On motion, the petitioner submitted published material not previously part of the record of proceedings, some of which was in a foreign language without English translations.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The petitioner failed to submit required English translations under 8 C.F.R. §§ 103.2(b)(3) and 204.5(h)(3). The petitioner also failed to submit evidence relating to the publications in which the articles appeared to establish that they constitute professional or major trade journals or other major media under 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(iii). The petitioner was on notice from the regulation and the director's denial that such evidence was required. Additionally, counsel's new assertion that the director erred in considering publication date could not be raised for the first time on motion to reconsider.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Evidence submitted in prior proceedings (not re-described in this motion decision) that established the petitioner's participation as a judge of the work of others.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO's prior decision of August 8, 2013 found that the petitioner established eligibility for the judging criterion under 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(iv). This was the only criterion the petitioner satisfied.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner had submitted evidence previously under this criterion, though counsel did not contest the AAO's findings on this criterion in the motion.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO's prior decision found that the petitioner had not submitted qualifying evidence under 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(vii), and counsel did not contest this finding on motion. The petitioner attempted to rely on expert opinion letters as 'comparable evidence' under 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(4), citing Muni v. INS and Matter of Price, but the AAO rejected this because comparable evidence is only permitted when the ten regulatory categories do not readily apply to the beneficiary's occupation, and the petitioner himself had claimed eligibility under nine of the ten categories, demonstrating that the categories were applicable.

outcome_reasoning: The AAO dismissed both the motion to reopen and the motion to reconsider. The motion to reopen was dismissed because the petitioner failed to submit evidence of new facts sufficient to overcome the deficiencies identified on appeal — much of the evidence was previously submitted, the new Embassy letter addressed only one award, foreign-language materials lacked required translations, and the petitioner failed to establish the publications were qualifying media. Even if the awards criterion were satisfied, the petitioner would still not meet at least three regulatory criteria. The motion to reconsider was dismissed because it raised new legal arguments that could have been raised earlier, attempted to resurrect abandoned criteria, and did not specify factual or legal errors or identify a change in law materially affecting the prior decision. The AAO also rejected the 'comparable evidence' argument under 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(4) because the ten criteria readily applied to the petitioner's occupation. The burden of proof under Section 291 of the Act was not met.
legal_citations: Section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act
Muni v. INS, 891 F. Supp. 440 (N.D. Ill. 1995)
Matter of Price, 20 I&N Dec. 953 (Assoc. Comm'r 1994)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: Procedural issues included: (1) the petitioner abandoned criteria (ii), (iii), (v), (viii), (ix), and (x) on appeal and attempted to resurrect them on motion, which is procedurally improper; (2) foreign-language published materials were submitted without required English translations under 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(3); (3) the petitioner submitted an unsigned May 5, 2012 letter purportedly from a declarant, which the AAO found had no probative value (though not characterized as fraud); (4) new legal arguments were improperly raised on motion to reconsider that could have been raised earlier; (5) evidence was offered for the first time before the AAO after the petitioner had been on notice of deficiencies during the RFE stage, barred under Matter of Soriano and Matter of Obaigbena.
